statement.markdown: Given a singly linked list and an integer `data`, insert a new node with the given `data` at the head of the linked list. The function should return the head of the modified linked list. The input linked list is represented as a sequence of integers, where each integer is the data of a node in the linked list. If the linked list is empty, the function should return a new node with the given `data`. The output should be the head of the modified linked list, represented as a sequence of integers.
statement.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer `n`, the number of nodes in the linked list. The second line contains `n` space-separated integers, representing the data of the nodes in the linked list. The third line contains an integer `data`, the data of the new node to be inserted at the head of the linked list.
statement.outputFormat: The output contains a sequence of integers, representing the data of the nodes in the modified linked list.
statement.notes: If the input linked list is empty, the function should return a new node with the given `data`.

constraints.description: The number of nodes in the linked list
The data of the new node to be inserted at the head of the linked list

examples.explanation: The new node with data 4 is inserted at the head of the linked list, resulting in the modified linked list 4 -> 1 -> 2 -> 3.
The input linked list is empty, so a new node with data 5 is created and returned as the head of the modified linked list.
The new node with data 30 is inserted at the head of the linked list, resulting in the modified linked list 30 -> 10 -> 20.
The new node with data 25 is inserted at the head of the linked list, resulting in the modified linked list 25 -> 50.

hints.text: first, you need to create a new node with the given data. this new node will be the head of the modified linked list.
then, you need to consider the case when the input linked list is empty. in this case, the new node with the given data will be the head of the linked list.
when the input linked list is not empty, you need to update the head of the linked list to point to the new node. to do this, you need to keep track of the current head of the linked list.
you can traverse the linked list to find the current head, but this approach is inefficient. instead, you can directly access the head of the linked list because it's given as a sequence of integers.

to update the head of the linked list, you need to create a new node with the given data and set its next pointer to the current head of the linked list. this way, the new node becomes the head of the modified linked list.

editorial.title: Efficient Insertion at Head of a Linked List
editorial.intuition: The problem requires inserting a new node at the head of a linked list. A brute force approach would involve traversing the entire list to find the head, which is inefficient. However, a key observation is that we can simply create a new node and make it point to the current head, effectively making the new node the new head.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve traversing the entire linked list to find the head, then inserting the new node. This approach is correct but has a time complexity of O(n), where n is the number of nodes in the list, because we have to traverse the entire list. This is slow for large lists.
editorial.betterSolution: The optimal approach involves directly creating a new node and making it the new head by pointing it to the current head.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution involves creating a new node with the given data and making it point to the current head of the list. This approach has a time complexity of O(1) because it involves a constant number of operations, regardless of the size of the list.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution can be proven by observing that the new node becomes the new head of the list, and the rest of the list remains unchanged. This is because we are simply updating the head pointer to point to the new node, and the new node's next pointer points to the current head.
editorial.dryRun: For example, if the list is 1 -> 2 -> 3 and we want to insert 0 at the head, the optimal solution would create a new node with data 0 and make it point to the current head (1). The resulting list would be 0 -> 1 -> 2 -> 3, which is correct.
commonMistakes: Forgetting to update the head pointer to point to the new node.
Trying to traverse the list to find the head, which is unnecessary.
Not handling the case where the list is empty.
Incorrectly updating the next pointer of the new node.
Not creating a new node with the given data.
editorial.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is the length of the original list
editorial.spaceComplexity: O(1) additional space (excluding input and output structures)

functionSignature.java: public ListNode insertAtHead(ListNode head, int data);
functionSignature.python: def insertAtHead(self, head: ListNode | None, data: int) -> ListNode:
